content.claim: Sweden's market-based and accountability-driven school reforms (beginning early 1990s) have produced faster and steeper segregation increases than Finland's more modest choice-expansion approach, suggesting that institutional guardrails (teacher professionalism norms, funding equalization, nearby-school enrollment defaults) can dampen but not eliminate choice-driven segregation even in nominally comprehensive systems
content.context: Fresh live research through the Finnish system reform lens
limitations: This is a comparative inference rather than a controlled causal test
Timing and magnitude of segregation increases are described qualitatively; quantitative comparisons of effect sizes not provided
Other differences between Swedish and Finnish institutional contexts (governance autonomy, teacher union power, funding mechanisms) are not fully accounted for in the comparison
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
